description: The provided image is unreadable and contains no discernible scientific content, appearing only as a generic yellow checkmark icon without resolvable text, axes, or microstructural features. Based on the accompanying text extraction summary, this figure is inferred to belong to the paper investigating Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel. The study characterizes an austenite-B2 dual-phase microstructure with dual-nanoprecipitation, specifically L'1_2 long-range ordered domains averaging 1.0 ± 0.3 nm and coherent VC carbides of 19.9 ± 6.2 nm distributed within the austenite matrix. The material achieves a yield strength of 1316 ± 16 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1458 ± 11 MPa, and total elongation of 11.7 ± 1.2 %. The underlying strengthening mechanisms include heterogeneous deformation-induced (HDI) stress contributing approximately 597 MPa, solid solution strengthening, Taylor dislocation hardening, and Orowan precipitation strengthening. Deformation behavior reveals three distinct work-hardening stages and progressive geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation. However, all microstructural details, quantitative data, and figure-specific conclusions are derived solely from the extracted text summary, as the image itself offers no visually verifiable information.

Figure 1 comprises two panels illustrating the processing route and equilibrium phase constitution of an Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni (wt.%) duplex lightweight steel. Panel (a) presents a temperature-time profile of the full thermomechanical processing sequence: homogenization at 1050 °C for 1 h, hot rolling with an 85% thickness reduction, water quenching, cold rolling with a 50% reduction, solution treatment at 900 °C for 15 min, and final water quenching. Panel (b) shows the calculated equilibrium phase diagram using Thermo-Calc 2021b, plotting volume fraction versus temperature for austenite, B2, κ-carbide, and VC. It reveals that at the 900 °C solution-treatment temperature the alloy consists predominantly of austenite (~78%) and B2 (~20%), with trace VC and essentially no κ-carbide. The κ-carbide phase is stable below approximately 780 °C but dissolves at higher temperatures, while the austenite fraction increases from ~56% at 500 °C to a plateau near 80% between 800 and 1000 °C before slightly declining. Together, the panels rationalize the selection of 900 °C for solution treatment to achieve a duplex austenite-plus-B2 microstructure free of κ-carbide, providing the thermodynamic and processing basis for the subsequent dual-nanoprecipitation and mechanical property design.

Figure 2 presents the XRD and EBSD characterization of the duplex lightweight steel after rolling and solution treatment. (a) The XRD pattern identifies diffraction peaks from austenite (γ), B2, and VC phases, with the B2 phase fraction quantified as 20%. (b) The EBSD phase map distinguishes the austenite matrix (green) from the B2 phase (red), confirming f_B2 = 20%. (c,d) Inverse pole figure (IPF) maps display the crystallographic orientations of austenite and B2 grains, respectively. (e) The austenite grain-size distribution follows a unimodal Gaussian fit with an average diameter (D̄_γ) of 1.31 μm. (f) The B2 phase shows a bimodal size distribution comprising fine band-like grains averaging 1.02 μm and coarser particles averaging 3.38 μm. These results collectively reveal a refined duplex microstructure with ultrafine austenite and a bimodal B2 phase, providing the structural basis for the alloy's ultrahigh strength and ductility.

This figure presents comprehensive (S)TEM characterization of the initial microstructure in an Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel. Subfigures (a1) through (a8) show STEM-EDS elemental maps for Fe, Mn, Al, C, V, and Ni, plus a composite overlay of Al, V, C, and Ni, revealing compositional partitioning between the austenite and B2 phases. (b) displays a high-resolution TEM image of the semi-coherent interface between the B2 and austenite (γ) phases, with the inset fast Fourier transform pattern indicating an orientation relationship of [011]γ // [111]B2. (c) provides an HRTEM image of an individual coherent VC precipitate within the austenite matrix, with the inset selected-area electron diffraction pattern confirming [011]γ // [011]VC. (d) is a dark-field TEM image taken from the L′12 superlattice reflection, verifying the presence of L′12-type long-range ordered nanodomains. (e) presents an inverse FFT image with the L′12 ordered nanodomains highlighted by red circles. Collectively, these results establish a duplex austenite-B2 microstructure featuring dual-nanoprecipitation of coherent VC carbides and ultrafine L′12 LRO domains, providing critical nanostructural evidence for the multiple strengthening mechanisms.

Figure 4 presents the tensile mechanical properties of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel. Panel (a) displays the engineering stress–strain curve, showing a yield strength of ~1316 MPa, an ultimate tensile strength of ~1458 MPa, and a total elongation of ~11.7%; the inset SEM fractograph (scale bar 5 μm) reveals a ductile dimpled fracture surface. Panel (b) shows the true stress–strain curve (black) and the corresponding work-hardening rate curve (red), which the authors divide into three stages. Stage I (~0–1% true strain) exhibits a sharp drop in work-hardening rate to below zero. Stage II (~1–4.3%) features a rapid increase to a peak near 3.7 GPa at ~4.3% true strain. Stage III (~4.3–12%) shows a gradual decrease while remaining above 2.7 GPa. The paper attributes Stage I to a large dislocation mean free path and interfacial stress relaxation after B2 yielding, Stage II to rapid dislocation storage and hetero-deformation induced (HDI) hardening, and Stage III to sustained HDI strengthening coupled with dynamic recovery and L'1_2 ordered-domain dissolution.

Figure 5 presents loading-unloading-reloading (LUR) tensile test data for an Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel, evaluating the Bauschinger effect and heterogeneous deformation induced (HDI) strengthening. Panel (a) displays the true stress versus true strain curve with six unloading-reloading cycles imposed at true strains of approximately 1.0%, 2.9%, 4.9%, 6.8%, 8.6%, and 10.4%. Panel (b) enlarges a representative hysteresis loop at 8.6% true strain, defining the unloaded yield stress (σ_u), reloaded yield stress (σ_r), yield drop (Δσ_y), and the HDI stress calculated as (σ_u + σ_r)/2. Panel (c) shows that both the hysteresis area and Δσ_y increase monotonically with true strain, indicating a pronounced Bauschinger effect. Panel (d) reveals that σ_HDI rises progressively from about 620 MPa to approximately 845 MPa as strain increases. These trends confirm that deformation incompatibility between soft austenite and hard B2 phases promotes geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation at phase boundaries, generating long-range back stresses and forward stresses that significantly contribute to work-hardening and HDI strengthening.

This figure presents geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) density distributions and their quantitative evolution in a duplex lightweight steel (Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni) under tensile strains of 0%, 1%, 3%, and 6%. Subfigures (a1–d1) show EBSD-based GND maps for the austenite (γ) phase, while (a2–d2) display those for the B2 phase, with the color scale ranging from 10^13 to 10^16 m^-2. As strain increases, both phases exhibit progressively higher GND densities, transitioning from blue (low density) toward yellow/green (high density). The frequency distribution plots in (e) for austenite and (f) for B2, fitted with Gaussian curves, yield average GND values increasing from 5.36×10^13 to 2.64×10^14 m^-2 in austenite and from 1.04×10^14 to 2.51×10^14 m^-2 in B2. Subfigure (g) directly compares these average values, revealing that although B2 starts with a higher GND density due to its smaller grain size, austenite accumulates dislocations more rapidly and surpasses B2 at 6% strain. Heterogeneous GND distributions within similarly sized grains (marked G1–G4 in c1–c2) indicate that factors beyond grain size govern local deformation. These results support co-deformation of both phases and provide evidence for hetero-deformation induced (HDI) hardening and forest dislocation strengthening.

This figure displays electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) Schmid factor distribution maps showing the evolution of austenite and B2 grains with increasing strain (0%, 1%, 3%, and 6%) in an Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel. Panels (a1) to (d1) correspond to the austenite (γ) phase with a color scale ranging from 0 to 0.5, while panels (a2) to (d2) represent the B2 phase with a scale from 0 to 0.2; all maps include 5 μm scale bars. The color variations within individual grains indicate orientation-dependent slip system activation in each phase. With progressive straining, the Schmid factor distributions evolve distinctly between the two phases, reflecting heterogeneous deformation and strain partitioning across the soft austenite matrix and the harder B2 phase. These maps provide direct microstructural evidence for cooperative deformation mechanisms and support the analysis of geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation and hetero-deformation induced (HDI) strengthening in the duplex steel.

Figure 8 presents TEM analyses of deformation-induced microstructures in an Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel at engineering strains of 6% (a–d) and 12% near final failure (e–h). Panels (a) and (e) are bright-field (BF) images showing increased dislocation density in both austenite and B2 phases with strain, with dislocations forming nonplanar slip bands and pile-ups around B2 particles. Panels (b) and (f) are dark-field (DF) images with selected-area diffraction patterns (SADP) inset, revealing attenuated superlattice reflections and reduced volume fraction of L1′2-type long-range ordered (LRO) domains within slip bands. Panels (c) and (g) show inverse FFT images confirming that dislocations shear through LRO domains, progressively destroying them. Panels (d) and (h) illustrate that coherent VC precipitates impede dislocation motion and cause dislocation accumulation, with the hindering effect becoming more pronounced at 12% strain. The figure demonstrates that austenite/B2 interfaces block planar slip and reflect dislocations, while dual-nanoprecipitation governs strain-hardening via dislocation shearing and pinning mechanisms.

Figure 9 presents a quantitative strengthening-contribution chart that decomposes the calculated yield strength (YS) of the investigated duplex lightweight steel into its individual components, with strength contribution in MPa on the y-axis. From left to right, the labeled bars represent: heterogeneous deformation-induced (HDI) stress σ_HDI (597 MPa), austenite lattice-friction stress weighted by phase fraction σ_0,γ f_γ (78 MPa), austenite solid-solution strengthening σ_ss,γ f_γ (182 MPa), austenite dislocation strengthening σ_dis,γ f_γ (85 MPa), VC precipitation strengthening σ_VC f_γ (74 MPa), L′1_2 long-range ordered domain strengthening σ_LRO f_γ (119 MPa), B2 lattice-friction stress σ_0,B2 f_B2 (135 MPa), and B2 dislocation strengthening σ_dis,B2 f_B2 (46 MPa). The rightmost stacked column labeled Exp. σ_YS visually sums these contributions to a total of 1316 MPa, consistent with the experimentally determined yield strength. The figure demonstrates that HDI strengthening provides the largest single contribution, while the combined synergy of dual-nanoprecipitation (VC and LRO domains), solid-solution, dislocation, and phase-specific strengthening mechanisms accounts for the ultrahigh yield strength achieved in the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni steel.
